course_title: Introduction to Philosophy: What is Philosophy?
course_description:
The questions of what philosophy is and what philosophers do are approached through a discussion of the work of philosophers such as Descartes, Locke, Hume, Sartre, Fanon, Beauvoir, and Merleau-Ponty. The first section of the course is devoted to questions of what there is (metaphysics) and what it is to know (epistemology), and topics covered include scepticism, the relationship between mind and body, and the problem of personal identity. The second section responds to earlier work on Descartes by providing an introduction to philosophy from the existential and phenomenological perspectives of embodiment, eroticism, inter-subjectivity, and love. It explores ideas of intentionality and the lived body, the ontological significance of the gaze, relations between the self and the other, and the relevance of these for a philosophical understanding of our everyday lives. Note: This course is available online, as well as face-to-face in Semester 1.

The questions of what philosophy is and what philosophers do are approached through a discussion of the work of philosophers such as Socrates, Mary Graham, Plato, Boethius, Aquinas, Descartes, Elizabeth of Bohemia, Hume, Camus, Sartre, Beauvoir, Fanon, Charles Mills, Merleau-Ponty, and Brendan Hokowhitu. The first section of this course is devoted to questions of what there is (metaphysics) and what it is to know (epistemology), and topics covered include scepticism, the relationship between mind and body (dualism and materialism), and the problem of the rationality of science. The second section of the course responds to (and challenges) earlier work on Descartes (and Cartesianism) by providing an introduction to European philosophy from the existential and phenomenological perspectives. It explores ideas of the meaning of life, our relations with others, embodiment, considerations of gender and race, and the relevance of these questions for a philosophical understanding of what it means to be a human.

course_aims: Philosophy uses critical thinking and logical argument to look at questions relating to the meaning of life, for example, the nature of the external world, the nature of human beings, knowledge, meaning, freedom, ethics and politics. This course introduces students to some of the basic questions of philosophy in the context of reading some of the major thinkers of modern philosophy, including Descartes, Locke, Hume, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, Fanon and Beauvoir. Students in this course are given an opportunity to develop their own ideas and their own voice and to understand the role of philosophical thinking in everyday life.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand some of the central debates in philosophy (breadth and depth)
Demonstrate basic skills of effective thinking and communication (clarity, relevance, accuracy)
Conduct independent philosophical research and inquiry
Identify, analyse and evaluate arguments or points of view
Understand the role of philosophy in everyday life

assessment_details.title: Critical reading task
assessment_details.task_description: Students will respond to set piece based on the philosophers of weeks 2-4 (Plato, Boethius, Aquinas). Students are required to write a 300 word analysis and critique of the piece, evaluating the content using their own individual judgement and perspective. Further details are available on the Blackboard site under 'Assessment'. Artificial Intelligence (AI) provides emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI use may constitute student misconduct under the student code of conduct. A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments must be submitted via TurnItIn (on Blackboard); please check that your submission has been received.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Essay One
assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to write a philosophical essay on a topic from Part I of the course. Topics are specified in detail in the essay task sheet on Blackboard. Maximum word count: 1000 words (includes discursive footnotes but excludes references and bibliography). For this assignment you may use any recognised referencing style, though you must be consistent throughout. A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: What is Philosophy? An introduction to philosophy and the course. Learning outcomes: L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 12
learning_activities.topic: 10 x one hour tutorials Choose one tutorial time from the options on Si-Net. Tutorials will be held weeks 2-7 and 9-12. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Love Plato's Symposium and the ladder of love. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Life Boethius and the consolation of philosophy. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and God Thomas Aquinas and theology. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Truth Descartes' Meditations, scepticism, and self-knowledge. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Reality Descartes' Meditations, the external world, and the mind/body problem. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Knowledge Hume's Essay, empiricism, and the problem of induction Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

Philosophy and Suspicion Introduction to 20th century European philosophy with Marx, Nietzsche, and Freud on false consciousness and the fate of philosophy without Truth. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Absurdity Albert Camus’ The Myth of Sisyphus on affirming life in the face of an absurd, meaningless universe. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

Philosophy and Freedom Introduction to existentialism with Jean-Paul Sartre’s “Existentialism is a Humanism” on human existence, freedom, and our relation to the Other. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Gender Feminist existentialism with Simone de Beauvoir’s The Second Sex and thinking about the philosophical condition of Woman under patriarchy. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Race Black existentialism with Franz Fanon’s Black Skin, White Masks, critiquing white philosophy and examining lived experience under racism. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

Philosophy and Embodiment Merleau-Ponty’s phenomenology of the body in The Phenomenology of Perception and thinking through the racialised body in Hokowhitu’s Indigenous existentialism. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
